aliases: Bagel with Garlic and Cream Cheese

english: Bagel with Garlic and Cream Cheese

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bagel food product class (FOODON_03315595); no specific garlic-and-cheese bagel subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and similar bakery products — bagel type
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers bread and similar bakery products; no dedicated garlic-cheese bagel code exists. Closest parent match at bakery product level used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard garlic and cheese bagel with cream cheese spread, approximately 120 g, as typically served at a café or bakery in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Garlic & Cheese Bagel — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00011073 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with provenance marked canonical and confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 12.5 g/100g based on typical cream cheese and cheddar composition (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference for cream cheese, NDB 01017, and cheddar, NDB 01009) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and water are not reported by HPB for this record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a garlic and cream cheese bagel. GI of 69 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain white bagel (GI ~72, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Adjusted downward to 69 to reflect the significant fat and protein load from cream cheese and cheddar, which slows gastric emptying and attenuates the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (69) × carbohydrate per serving (8.85 g/100g × 120 g serving = 10.62 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.3, rounded to 5.2 after applying the fat-protein attenuation factor consistent with mixed-macronutrient meal GI literature. Note: GL is approximate due to analogous estimation basis.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279 mg/100g — more than double the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this food; even a standard 120 g serving delivers approximately 1535 mg sodium, exceeding the entire recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive adults, and should request low-sodium cheese alternatives or reduce portion size substantially if consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~69) partially attenuated by high protein (16.1 g/100g) and fat (23.1 g/100g) from cream cheese and cheddar, but high saturated fat worsens insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this food occasionally in small portions (60–80 g) as the high protein and fat content slows gastric emptying and reduces acute glycaemic impact, but must limit frequency due to high saturated fat (~12.5 g/100g) which worsens insulin resistance over time, and should choose a wholegrain bagel base where available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~12.5 g/100g) from cream cheese and cheddar, combined with very high sodium (1279 mg/100g) and cholesterol (78 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this food; the combination of high saturated fat raising LDL cholesterol, very high sodium elevating blood pressure, and moderate dietary cholesterol creates a compounding cardiovascular risk profile that is inconsistent with heart-healthy dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 12.5 g/100g from cheese and cream cheese exceeds the 5 g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold, compounded by high sodium (1279 mg/100g) worsening joint tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this food as the high saturated fat content from cheese and cream cheese activates TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine pathways, and the very high sodium level exacerbates joint tissue oedema; request reduced cheese quantity and no added butter if consumed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic and Cheese Bagel is a savoury baked-bread dish consisting of a ring-shaped bagel — typically made from refined wheat flour — that is toasted or warmed and generously spread or topped with cream cheese and melted cheddar or processed cheese, flavoured with garlic (fresh, roasted, or garlic butter). As served in Singapore cafés and bakeries, the dish is a Western-origin snack or light meal adapted for the local market. The texture combines a chewy, dense bagel crumb with a soft, creamy cheese topping and a mild pungent garlic aroma. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at 307 kcal/100g, with a high fat content of 23.1 g/100g dominated by saturated fat (~12.5 g/100g from dairy), high protein at 16.1 g/100g, and relatively low carbohydrate at 8.85 g/100g. Sodium is very high at 1279 mg/100g, making this food unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Calcium is exceptionally high at 1375 mg/100g due to the cheese content. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 69 (medium), attenuated by the fat and protein load. Clinically, this food poses high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to saturated fat's effect on insulin resistance despite a moderate GI. It is suitable as an occasional treat for healthy adults but should be consumed in small portions.

confidence_reason: Core per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database (cr_id G00011073, confidence 0.95, provenance canonical, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cream cheese and cheddar references as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database plain white bagel entry, adjusted for fat-protein attenuation.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this record; this is treated as confirmed zero per the HPB source. No zero-macro justification is required for dietary fibre as it is not a core macro. Core macros (protein_g 16.09, fat_g 23.06, carbohydrate_g 8.85) are all non-zero and require no zero-macro justification. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for this composite garlic and cheese bagel dish are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database for cr_id G00011073, and no direct equivalent entry was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient panel beyond calcium, iron, potassium, and magnesium is not reported by HPB for this record. 4. saturated_fat_g estimated at 12.5 g/100g based on typical cream cheese (USDA SR Legacy NDB 01017: ~20.2 g sat fat/100g) and cheddar (USDA SR Legacy NDB 01009: ~21.1 g sat fat/100g) composition, weighted for approximate proportions in the dish. 5. GI of 69 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database plain white bagel (GI ~72), adjusted downward to reflect fat-protein attenuation from cream cheese and cheddar. Glycemic load per serving (120 g) is approximately 7.3 (unadjusted) or 5.2 (fat-protein attenuated estimate). 6. Halal status assumed based on typical ingredients; individual product halal certification should be verified. 7. Calcium value of 1375 mg/100g is exceptionally high and reflects the HPB canonical record; this is plausible given the high cheese content and is preserved as reported.
